title: Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Alright, let's get this farm going. First, find the main game board. This is the rectangular board showing all the action spaces. Place it in the center of the table where both players can comfortably reach it. This board is where you'll send your workers each round to gather resources, build things, and collect animals.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a Farm Board
walkthrough.text: Each player gets one farm board. These are the small rectangular boards that represent your personal farm. Orient them so the forest is at the top and the path is at the bottom. Your farm starts with 9 spaces in a 3x3 grid, and your cottage is already printed on one of them. This is your home base for the whole game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Special Buildings
walkthrough.text: Find the 4 special building tiles: the Half-Timbered House, Open Stables, Shelter, and Storage Building. Lay them face up next to the game board where both players can see them. These are buildings you can construct during the game using the Special Building action spaces. Each one has a unique cost and ability printed on it.

walkthrough.title: Set Aside Expansions, Troughs, and Stalls
walkthrough.text: Place the 4 farm expansion tiles, 10 feeding troughs, and 4 stall tiles off to the side. These form a supply that both players can claim during the game. Important: once all of any type are taken, no more become available. The stall tiles have stables on the reverse side, so you will flip them later when you upgrade.

walkthrough.title: Sort Building Materials and Animals
walkthrough.text: Sort all the wooden pieces into groups to form the general supply. You should have: 17 wood tokens, 15 stone tokens, and 5 reed tokens for building materials. For animals, you have 22 sheep, 16 pigs, 13 cows, and 14 horses. Also set the 9 goods tiles nearby. These cardboard tiles represent bundles of 4 and can substitute when individual pieces run low.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Borders to Players
walkthrough.text: Give each player 9 yellow border pieces. These are the fences and walls you'll use to enclose pastures on your farm. Place the remaining 8 borders next to the game board near the Expand action space. One border gets added to the Expand space each round, so these 8 borders also serve as a round tracker. When they run out, the game is over.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player Their Workers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes 3 workers in their color. One player gets blue, the other gets red. These workers are the heart of the game. Each round, you'll take turns placing them on action spaces to do things. Three workers means three actions per round for each player, six total placements per round.

walkthrough.title: Refill the Starting Action Spaces
walkthrough.text: Before round 1 begins, place starting goods on the action spaces that have refill arrows. Look at each space with an arrow and add the goods shown. For example, place 1 wood on the Start Player space, 3 wood on the 3 Wood space, 1 stone on the 1 Stone space, 2 stone on the 2 Stone space, and 1 reed on the Millpond space. These accumulate each round if nobody takes them.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Start Player
walkthrough.text: Pick who goes first by any method you like. Give that player the start player tile. Unlike many games, the start player does not automatically rotate in this game. You have to specifically choose the Start Player action space on the game board to claim the tile and go first next round. Once you have your start player, you are ready to begin. Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Place the main game board centrally between both players
All action spaces are printed on this board

walkthrough.title: Game Structure: 8 Rounds, 4 Phases
walkthrough.text: The game is played over exactly 8 rounds. You can track which round you are in by counting how many yellow borders have been added to the Expand space, since one is added each round. Each round has 4 phases that happen in order: Refill, Work, Home, and Breeding. Let's walk through each phase.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Refill
walkthrough.text: At the start of each round, you refill certain action spaces on the board. Look for spaces with a refill arrow on them. Add the printed goods to those spaces, even if goods from previous rounds are still sitting there. Goods accumulate, making spaces more valuable the longer they go unclaimed. Spaces without the refill arrow do not get any goods added. Also add 1 yellow border from the supply to the Expand action space.

walkthrough.title: Refill Detail: Good 1 vs Good 2
walkthrough.text: Some refill spaces show a format like 'good 1 (good 2)'. This means you add good 1 if the space is currently empty, but add good 2 if the space still has goods on it. For example, the Millpond space shows '1 reed (1 sheep)'. In round 1, you place 1 reed. If nobody takes it, in round 2 you add 1 sheep instead. Once someone takes everything, it resets to 1 reed next round.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Work — Placing Workers
walkthrough.text: Starting with the start player, you take turns placing exactly one worker on an available action space. As soon as you place your worker, you immediately carry out that action. Then your opponent places a worker and carries out their action. You alternate until all 6 workers have been placed, 3 from each player. This is the meat of the game.

walkthrough.title: Worker Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: Here is the critical rule: each action space can only hold one worker per round. Once a space is occupied, it is blocked for the rest of the round. This is where the tension comes from. If your opponent grabs the action you wanted, you need a backup plan. You are also not allowed to choose an action space without carrying out the associated action. If an action space has goods on it, you must take all of them when you place your worker there.

walkthrough.title: What Happens to Resources and Animals
walkthrough.text: Building materials like wood, stone, and reed go into your personal supply when you collect them. You can spend them later on building actions. Animals, however, must be placed on your farm board immediately. If you cannot house an animal because you have no room, it runs away and goes back to the general supply. This is a big deal. Always make sure you have pastures, stalls, or other housing ready before collecting animals.

walkthrough.title: Keeping Animals in Pastures
walkthrough.text: You use your yellow border pieces to enclose pastures on your farm. A fully enclosed pasture can hold up to 2 animals per space it covers. Each pasture can only hold one type of animal. You can have unfinished pastures with gaps in the borders, but they cannot hold any animals until fully enclosed. It does not matter whether borders are wood fences or stone walls; they work the same way.

walkthrough.title: Keeping Animals in Buildings
walkthrough.text: Animals can also live in buildings. Your cottage can hold exactly 1 animal. A stall can hold up to 3 animals of the same type. A stable, which is an upgraded stall, can hold up to 5 animals of the same type. Special buildings have their own capacity printed on the tile. Buildings also help enclose pastures because each building tile has borders printed around it on all 4 sides.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Troughs
walkthrough.text: Feeding troughs are incredibly powerful. They double the animal capacity of whatever they are in. A pasture space that holds 2 animals holds 4 with a trough. A stall that holds 3 holds 6. Your cottage that holds 1 holds 2. You can place one feeding trough per farm space, but a multi-space pasture can have multiple troughs. A free-standing trough on a space that is not part of a pasture or building can hold exactly 1 animal next to it.

walkthrough.title: Building Stalls and Stables
walkthrough.text: A stall costs 3 stone and 1 reed to build. You place it on any empty farm space that does not already have a building. A stall holds 3 animals of the same type. To upgrade a stall to a stable, you pay either 5 wood or 5 stone, not a mix. Just flip the stall tile over. A stable holds 5 animals of the same type. If the stall had a feeding trough, the trough stays when you upgrade.

walkthrough.title: The Action Spaces Overview
walkthrough.text: The game board has action spaces organized into several categories. Resource spaces let you collect wood, stone, reed, and animals. Building spaces let you construct fences, walls, stalls, feeding troughs, and special buildings. The Expand space gives you a farm expansion and borders. And the Start Player space gives you the first-player tile plus wood. Let's cover the key ones.

walkthrough.title: Resource Action Spaces
walkthrough.text: Start Player and 1 Wood: Take the start player tile and all wood on this space. 3 Wood: Take all wood from this space. 1 Stone: Take all stone. 2 Stone: Take all stone. Building Materials: Take 1 wood, 1 stone, and 1 reed from the general supply, not a refill space. Millpond: Take the reed and all sheep from this space. Animal spaces like Pigs and Sheep, Cows and Pigs, and Horses and Sheep give you all the animals shown.

walkthrough.title: Building Action Spaces
walkthrough.text: Fences: Add as many borders as you want from your personal supply to your farm. Pay 1 wood per border. Walls: Add as many borders as you want. The first 2 are free, then pay 2 stone for each additional border. Stalls: Build exactly 1 stall for 3 stone and 1 reed. Feeding Trough: Build 1 feeding trough for free, plus additional troughs for 3 wood each. Stables: Pay 5 wood or 5 stone to upgrade a stall to a stable. You can repeat this action for multiple upgrades.

walkthrough.title: Expand and Special Building Spaces
walkthrough.text: Expand: Take a farm expansion tile and attach it to the left or right of your farm so the edges match. You also receive all the yellow borders sitting on that space, but you may not place those borders during this action. Special Building: There are two of these spaces, meaning up to 2 workers per round can build special buildings. Pay the cost shown on the building tile and add it to any empty farm space on your farm. You may only build 1 special building per action.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Home
walkthrough.text: After all 6 workers have been placed, the work phase ends. All workers return to their respective farms. Simple as that. Get ready for animals to breed.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Breeding
walkthrough.text: At the end of each round, your animals breed. If you have at least 2 animals of the same type anywhere on your farm, you receive 1 new animal of that type. You can get at most 1 new sheep, 1 new pig, 1 new cow, and 1 new horse per breeding phase. Even if you have 4 sheep in two different pastures, you still only get 1 baby sheep. Newborn animals that you do not have room for run away to the general supply.

walkthrough.title: Moving Animals
walkthrough.text: You can rearrange your animals on your farm at any time, not just on your turn. This is important for making room before collecting new animals or before breeding. The only rule is that each pasture holds one type, each building holds one type, and you respect all capacity limits. Get creative with shuffling animals around to maximize your space.

walkthrough.title: Permanent Placement Rules
walkthrough.text: Once anything is built on your farm, it cannot be moved or removed. Borders, buildings, feeding troughs, and farm expansions are all permanent. So plan carefully where you place things. The one exception is the Open Stables special building, which returns the stall tile it replaces to the general supply. Animals, however, can always be moved around freely.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: After 8 rounds, the game ends. You score points in several categories. First, count all your animals. Each animal is worth 1 point. Then apply the bonus and penalty table: you lose 3 points for each animal type where you have 3 or fewer. After 13 sheep, 11 pigs, 10 cows, or 9 horses, each additional animal of that type scores 1 extra bonus point. Next, add points from your buildings: each building tile shows its point value. Finally, each fully used farm expansion scores 4 points. A farm space counts as used if it has a building, a feeding trough, or is part of a fully enclosed pasture.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: Add up all your points from animals, bonuses, penalties, buildings, and farm expansions. The player with the most points wins. In case of a tie, the player who was not the start player at the beginning of round 1 wins. That is the entire game. Eight rounds of worker placement, animal management, and farm building.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Farm
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first round with two players: Alice (blue) and Bob (red). Alice is the start player. Each player has 3 workers and 9 borders. The board is refilled, animals are sorted, and we are ready to farm. Follow along to see how the game flows and learn from a couple of common mistakes.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Refill
walkthrough.text: Before anyone places workers, we do the refill phase. Place 1 wood on the Start Player space, 3 wood on the 3 Wood space, 1 stone on the 1 Stone space, 2 stone on the 2 Stone space, and 1 reed on the Millpond. Also add 1 yellow border to the Expand space. There are now goods waiting on several action spaces. Whoever grabs them gets everything sitting there.

walkthrough.title: Alice's First Action: 3 Wood
walkthrough.text: Alice goes first and sends a worker to the 3 Wood space. She takes all 3 wood tokens and adds them to her supply. Wood is essential for building fences and feeding troughs, so stocking up early is a solid opening move. She is thinking ahead about enclosing a pasture soon.

walkthrough.title: Bob's First Action: The Mistake — Grabbing Animals Too Early
walkthrough.text: Bob looks at the Pigs and Sheep space and sees a pig and a sheep sitting there. Free animals! He sends a worker and takes both. But wait. Bob has no pastures enclosed, no stalls built, and no feeding troughs. His cottage can hold only 1 animal. He places the sheep in his cottage, but the pig? There is nowhere to put it. The pig runs away back to the general supply. Bob just wasted half his action.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Build Housing Before Collecting Animals
walkthrough.text: This is the number one beginner mistake in Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small. Animals look tempting, but if you have nowhere to put them, they vanish. Always make sure you have pastures, stalls, or at least a feeding trough ready before you grab animals. The cottage only holds 1 animal, so it fills up immediately. Plan your housing first, then collect.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Second Action: Fences
walkthrough.text: Alice sends her second worker to the Fences action space. She spends 3 of her wood to place 3 borders from her personal supply onto her farm, partially enclosing a 2-space pasture. She knows she will need one more border to close it off, but she is setting things up for next round. Smart planning.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Second Action: The Mistake — Building a Stall Without Resources
walkthrough.text: Bob wants to fix his housing problem, so he looks at the Stalls action space. A stall costs 3 stone and 1 reed. Bob checks his supply: he has zero stone and zero reed. He spent his first action on animals instead of gathering materials. He cannot build the stall. He is forced to choose a different action. Bob sends his worker to the 2 Stone space instead, collecting 2 stone. Better than nothing, but he is a full round behind on his farm development.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Gather Resources Before You Need Them
walkthrough.text: Bob's second mistake cascaded from his first. By grabbing animals on turn 1 instead of resources, he had nothing to build with on turn 2. In this game, resources come first, buildings come second, and animals come third. You need wood and stone before you can fence or build, and you need fences and buildings before animals are useful. Think one or two steps ahead.

walkthrough.title: Alice's Third Action: Building Materials
walkthrough.text: Alice places her last worker on the Building Materials space and takes 1 wood, 1 stone, and 1 reed from the general supply. This is not a refill space, so it always gives the same thing. Alice now has a nice stockpile: 1 wood, 1 stone, and 1 reed. She is well-positioned to build a stall or extend her fences next round.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Third Action: 1 Stone
walkthrough.text: Bob sends his last worker to the 1 Stone space, collecting 1 stone. He now has 3 stone total. If he can get a reed next round, he can build a stall. Bob is recovering, but he lost tempo compared to Alice. The lesson is sinking in.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Workers Come Home
walkthrough.text: All 6 workers have been placed. The work phase is over. Both players take their workers back. Alice retrieves her 3 blue workers, Bob retrieves his 3 red workers. All action spaces are now clear and will be available again next round.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Breeding
walkthrough.text: Now we check for breeding. Alice has no animals at all, so nothing happens for her. Bob has 1 sheep in his cottage. Breeding requires at least 2 of the same type, so Bob gets no babies either. In the early rounds, breeding rarely triggers since you are still building your farm. But by round 3 or 4, once you have pairs of animals, breeding becomes a free animal every single round. That is huge.

walkthrough.title: Bob's Mistake: Not Claiming Start Player
walkthrough.text: Notice that neither player took the Start Player action this round. That means Alice keeps the start player tile and goes first again in round 2. Going first is a real advantage because you get first pick of all the freshly refilled spaces. Bob could have taken the Start Player space to grab 1 wood and secure first position next round, but he needed the stone more. Choosing when to take start player is a real strategic decision.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Start Player Is Valuable
walkthrough.text: The Start Player space gives you first pick of action spaces next round plus free wood. In a game where every action space can only be used once, going first is powerful. Do not ignore it for the entire game. Look for rounds where you can afford to take it without falling behind on resources.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Recap
walkthrough.text: After round 1, Alice has 1 wood, 1 stone, 1 reed, and a partially fenced pasture. She is set up to finish her pasture and start collecting animals next round. Bob has 3 stone, 1 sheep in his cottage, and a hard lesson learned. He will spend round 2 catching up on resources and housing. The difference? Alice planned her resource gathering before her animal collection.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have just walked through a complete first round of Agricola: All Creatures Big and Small. You have seen how worker placement works, learned why building housing before collecting animals matters, and watched how poor resource planning can set you back. Remember: resources first, housing second, animals third. And keep an eye on that Start Player space.

walkthrough.title: Before You Farm
walkthrough.text:
Before you jump into a full game, here are three things to keep in mind.

First — check out General Tips for beginner strategy and the most common traps to avoid.

Second — browse the Appendix for a quick reference on every action space, building, and rule.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun building your farm!

summary.title: Before You Farm

Browse the Appendix for quick reference on action spaces and buildings
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime — tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Resources Before Animals
walkthrough.text: The single most important principle in this game: collect building materials and build housing before you chase animals. Animals with no home run away. Every lost animal is a lost point. In rounds 1 and 2, focus on stockpiling wood and stone, building your first fences, and maybe getting a stall down. Animals will come naturally from breeding once you have a pair, so there is no rush. The player who builds first and collects second almost always wins.

walkthrough.title: Border Budget Management
walkthrough.text: You only get 9 borders to start, and the Expand space adds 1 per round. That is a very tight budget for 8 rounds. Every border counts. Think about efficiency: a 2-space pasture enclosed by a building on one side uses fewer borders than one fully fenced in the open. Plan your pasture layouts to share borders between adjacent pastures. Building a stall or special building adjacent to partial fencing can close off a pasture cheaply since buildings have printed borders on all sides.

walkthrough.title: Feeding Troughs Are Overpowered
walkthrough.text: Feeding troughs double animal capacity. A 2-space pasture normally holds 4 animals. Add one trough and it holds 8. Add a second trough in the other space and it holds 16 of the same type. In buildings, a stall goes from 3 to 6 and a stable from 5 to 10 with a trough. The Feeding Trough action gives you the first one free. This is one of the best action spaces in the game. Grab it early and often.

walkthrough.title: Get Breeding Started Early
walkthrough.text: Breeding gives you a free animal of each type every round, as long as you have at least 2 of that type. If you get a pair of sheep by round 3, that pair turns into 3 by round 4, then 4 by round 5, and so on. Over 5 rounds of breeding, one pair turns into 7 animals without spending a single action. Start with cheap animal types early like sheep from the Millpond and build pairs as fast as possible.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Neglect Any Animal Type
walkthrough.text: The scoring penalty is harsh: you lose 3 points for each animal type where you have 3 or fewer. That means having zero horses costs you the same as having 3 horses in the penalty. Since there are 4 animal types, ignoring just one type costs you 3 points. Ignoring two types costs you 6. Even getting just 4 of each type avoids all penalties. Make sure you pick up at least a pair of each animal type by mid-game so breeding can do the rest.

walkthrough.title: Watch What Your Opponent Is Doing
walkthrough.text: This is a 2-player game, so every action space your opponent takes is one you cannot use. Pay attention to their farm. If they need wood and the 3 Wood space just refilled, consider whether you should grab it before they do, even if it is not your top priority. Blocking a critical action can be worth more than taking your ideal action. Also watch for opportunities when they take an action you do not need, freeing you to execute your plan uncontested.

walkthrough.title: Farm Expansions: Timing Matters
walkthrough.text: Each farm expansion adds 3 new spaces and is worth 4 points if fully used. That is a great deal, but only if you can actually fill all 3 spaces with buildings, troughs, or enclosed pastures. An unused expansion scores zero. Grab an expansion in rounds 2 through 4 when you still have time to develop those spaces. Taking one in round 7 usually means at least one space goes unused. Also remember: you receive the borders on the Expand space but cannot place them in the same action.

walkthrough.title: Special Buildings: Know Your Options
walkthrough.text: The four special buildings each serve different strategies. The Half-Timbered House costs 3 wood, 2 stone, 1 reed and scores 5 points. It replaces your cottage and holds 2 animals. That is the highest raw point value. The Storage Building rewards hoarding building materials. The Shelter gives you an immediate free animal. The Open Stables cost 3 wood or 3 stone, score 2 points, hold 5 animals, and give you an immediate horse or cow. Pick the ones that fit your farm layout and animal plan.

summary.title: Resources Before Animals
bullets: Build housing before collecting animals — lost animals = lost points
Rounds 1-2: stockpile wood and stone, build fences
Animals come free from breeding once you have pairs

summary.title: Border Budget Management
bullets: Start with 9 borders + 1 per round from Expand = tight budget
Share borders between adjacent pastures for efficiency
Buildings have printed borders — use them to close pasture gaps cheaply

summary.title: Feeding Troughs Are Overpowered
bullets: Troughs double capacity: pasture space 2→4, stall 3→6, stable 5→10
2 troughs on a 2-space pasture = 16 animals of one type
Feeding Trough action gives first one free — grab it early

summary.title: Get Breeding Started Early
bullets: 2+ of a type = 1 free baby per round
Pair by round 3 → 7 animals by round 8 with no extra actions
Start with cheap types (sheep from Millpond) to build pairs fast

summary.title: Do Not Neglect Any Animal Type
bullets: -3 points for each animal type with 3 or fewer
Ignoring one type = -3; ignoring two = -6
Even 4 of each type avoids all penalties
Get at least a pair of each by mid-game for breeding

summary.title: Watch What Your Opponent Is Doing
bullets: 2-player game: every action they take blocks you
Consider blocking their critical actions even if not your top priority
Watch for open opportunities when they go elsewhere

summary.title: Farm Expansions: Timing Matters
bullets: 4 points per fully used expansion (all 3 spaces used)
Unused expansion = 0 points — grab by round 4 for time to develop
You get the borders on the Expand space but cannot place them that same action

summary.title: Special Buildings: Know Your Options
bullets: Half-Timbered House: 5 pts, replaces cottage, holds 2 animals (3 wood, 2 stone, 1 reed)
Storage Building: points based on leftover building materials
Shelter: 0 pts, gives 1 free animal immediately, holds 1 animal (2 wood, 1 stone)
Open Stables: 2 pts, holds 5 animals, gives 1 free horse or cow (3 wood or 3 stone)

walkthrough.title: The Sheep Engine
walkthrough.text: Sheep are the easiest animal to mass-produce because the Millpond gives you sheep on top of reed, and sheep appear on two animal action spaces: Pigs and Sheep, and Horses and Sheep. A strong sheep strategy starts by getting 2 sheep in a pasture with a feeding trough by round 2 or 3. Breeding kicks in immediately, and you will be swimming in sheep by mid-game. Since the bonus scoring threshold for sheep is 13, this strategy aims for 14 or more sheep to push into bonus territory while also collecting the other types to avoid penalties.

walkthrough.title: The Horse and Cow Investment
walkthrough.text: Horses and cows are scarcer and appear on fewer action spaces, but their bonus thresholds are lower: 9 for horses and 10 for cows. This means you need fewer to start earning bonus points. A focused horse or cow strategy uses the Open Stables special building, which gives a free horse or cow immediately and holds 5 animals. Pair it with a feeding trough for 10 capacity. Get a breeding pair going and you hit the bonus threshold faster than with sheep.

walkthrough.title: The Storage Building Gambit
walkthrough.text: The Storage Building scores points based on building materials in your supply at game end. Every 2 resources equals 1 point. This creates an unusual strategy: hoard resources instead of spending them all. The trick is to collect lots of wood and stone throughout the game while spending just enough to build the Storage Building itself and basic housing for animals. The Building Materials action space is your best friend here since it gives 1 of each resource type. Combined with unclaimed refill spaces that accumulate goods, you can end the game with 10 or more resources for 5 bonus points.

walkthrough.title: The Half-Timbered House Rush
walkthrough.text: The Half-Timbered House is worth 5 points, the most of any building. It costs 3 wood, 2 stone, and 1 reed, and replaces your cottage. Rushing to build it in rounds 2 or 3 gives you 5 guaranteed points early and upgrades your cottage to hold 2 animals. A typical rush line is: round 1, take 3 Wood and Building Materials. Round 2, take more stone and build the Half-Timbered House. You sacrifice some early animal collection, but the 5 points plus extra housing make up for it.

walkthrough.title: Pasture Efficiency and Layout
walkthrough.text: Advanced players think carefully about farm layout before placing a single border. The most efficient pasture is a large multi-space one because you use fewer total borders per space compared to many small pastures. However, each pasture only holds one animal type, so you need at least 4 separate areas for your 4 types. The ideal layout uses buildings to close off pasture edges, saving borders. A stall placed between two partial pastures can complete both of them at once. Plan your entire farm layout before round 1 ends.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Expand Action
walkthrough.text: The Expand action grows in value each round because 1 new border is added to it during each Refill phase. In round 1, you get the expansion plus only 1 border. By round 4, you get the expansion plus 4 accumulated borders, which is a massive haul. The tradeoff is that waiting too long means you may not fully use the expansion for the 4-point bonus. The sweet spot is usually rounds 3 or 4: enough accumulated borders to be efficient, enough remaining rounds to develop the 3 new spaces.

walkthrough.title: Blocking and Counter-Blocking
walkthrough.text: With only 2 players, blocking is incredibly potent. If your opponent desperately needs the Stalls action because they have 3 stone and 1 reed ready, you can take it just to deny them. This is especially powerful with the Start Player action. Going first lets you block their most critical space before they can act. Advanced players watch their opponent's supply, partially built pastures, and which animal types they are missing. If they are about to complete a critical build, consider whether denying it is worth more than your own ideal action.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Scoring Optimization
walkthrough.text: In rounds 7 and 8, shift your focus entirely to points. Count your animals in each type. If you have 3 or fewer of any type, grabbing even 1 more to reach 4 avoids the -3 penalty, which is effectively a 4-point swing. Check if any farm expansion spaces are unused. Can you throw a feeding trough on a space to make the expansion count? Look at building opportunities: even a 0-point Shelter still counts as using a farm space and gives you a free animal. Every point matters in a close game.

walkthrough.title: The Walls vs Fences Decision
walkthrough.text: Fences cost 1 wood each, and Walls give the first 2 free then charge 2 stone each. If you need 1 to 2 borders, Walls is almost always better because you get them free. For 3 borders, Fences costs 3 wood while Walls costs 2 stone for the same result. For 4 or more, Fences becomes more wood-efficient. Since stone is generally harder to get than wood, most players use Walls for small builds and Fences for large enclosures. Being flexible with both action spaces maximizes your border placement across the game.

walkthrough.title: Winning the End-Game Tiebreaker
walkthrough.text: In a tight game, the tiebreaker goes to the player who was not the start player in round 1. This means if you went second at the start, you have a small safety net. But more importantly, the start player tile is a strategic tool throughout the game. Taking Start Player in round 7 guarantees first pick in the critical final round. Some advanced players deliberately take Start Player in round 6 or 7 to ensure they can execute their scoring plan unblocked in the final round.

summary.title: The Sheep Engine
bullets: Sheep are the most available animal (Millpond, Pigs & Sheep, Horses & Sheep)
Get a breeding pair by round 2-3 with a trough for capacity
Bonus threshold: +1 per sheep past 13 — aim for 14+
Still collect other types to avoid -3 penalties

summary.title: The Horse and Cow Investment
bullets: Lower bonus thresholds: horses 9, cows 10
Open Stables: free horse or cow + 5 capacity (with trough = 10)
Fewer sources but easier to hit bonus territory
Pairs breed to threshold faster than sheep

summary.title: The Storage Building Gambit
bullets: Storage Building: every 2 resources in your supply = 1 point
Hoard resources while spending just enough for housing
Building Materials action (1w + 1s + 1r) is key
Can earn 5+ bonus points with 10+ leftover resources

summary.title: The Half-Timbered House Rush
bullets: 5 points — highest single building value
Cost: 3 wood, 2 stone, 1 reed — replaces cottage, holds 2 animals
Rush by rounds 2-3 for guaranteed early points
Typical line: 3 Wood + Building Materials round 1, build round 2

summary.title: Pasture Efficiency and Layout
bullets: Large pastures use fewer borders per space than small ones
Need 4 separate areas for 4 animal types
Use buildings to close pasture edges and save borders
Plan your entire layout before placing borders

summary.title: Timing the Expand Action
bullets: Expand gains +1 border per round via Refill
Round 1 = 1 border; Round 4 = 4 borders — big difference
Sweet spot: rounds 3-4 (enough borders + enough time to develop)
Unused expansion = 0 points — don't wait too long

summary.title: Blocking and Counter-Blocking
bullets: 2-player game makes blocking very powerful
Watch opponent's supply and partially built structures
Denying a critical action can be worth more than your best action
Start Player lets you block first next round

summary.title: Late-Game Scoring Optimization
bullets: Rounds 7-8: shift focus entirely to points
Going from 3 to 4 of an animal type = +4 point swing (avoid -3 penalty + gain 1)
Throw a trough on an unused expansion space to score the 4-point bonus
Shelter gives a free animal and uses a farm space

summary.title: The Walls vs Fences Decision
bullets: Fences: 1 wood per border — better for large builds (3+)
Walls: first 2 free, then 2 stone each — better for small builds (1-2)
Stone is generally harder to get than wood
Stay flexible and use both action spaces across the game

summary.title: Winning the End-Game Tiebreaker
bullets: Tiebreaker: player who was NOT start player in round 1 wins
Taking Start Player in round 7 guarantees first pick in final round
Advanced players plan Start Player claims around critical scoring rounds

entries.definition: Each player has 3 wooden workers in their color (blue or red). Workers are placed on action spaces during the Work Phase to carry out actions. All workers return home at the end of each round.
Each player's personal 3x3 grid of farm spaces. Starts with a cottage pre-printed on one space. Can be expanded with farm expansion tiles attached to the left or right side.
The central board showing all available action spaces. Placed between both players. Some spaces have refill arrows indicating goods are added each round.

Yellow wooden pieces used to enclose pastures on your farm. Each player starts with 9. Additional borders come from the Expand action space. Wood fences and stone walls function identically as borders.

Yellow wooden token placed on a farm space. Doubles the animal capacity of the pasture or building on that space. Maximum 1 per farm space. A free-standing trough (not in a pasture or building) holds exactly 1 animal. There are 10 in the game.

9 cardboard tiles representing bundles of 4 resources. Used when individual wooden pieces run low. Can be exchanged for individual goods at any time. Includes tiles for 4 sheep, 4 pigs, 4 cows, 4 horses, 4 wood, 4 stone, and 4 reed.
The player holding this tile goes first in each Work Phase. Does not rotate automatically. Must be claimed by choosing the Start Player action space.

A tile adding 3 new spaces to your farm, attached to the left or right side. Worth 4 points if all 3 spaces are fully used (each has a building, trough, or is part of a fully enclosed pasture). There are 4 expansions in the game.
Your starting building, pre-printed on your farm board. Counts as a special building. Holds exactly 1 animal (or 2 with a feeding trough). Can be upgraded to the Half-Timbered House.

A building tile that holds up to 3 animals of the same type (6 with a feeding trough). Costs 3 stone and 1 reed to build. Can be upgraded to a stable by paying 5 wood or 5 stone. Has borders printed on all 4 sides. There are 4 stall tiles in the game.

An upgraded stall, created by flipping the stall tile. Holds up to 5 animals of the same type (10 with a feeding trough). Upgrade costs 5 wood or 5 stone (not a mix). Keeps any feeding trough that was on the stall.

Special building. Cost: 3 wood, 2 stone, 1 reed. Worth 5 points. Must be built over the cottage (cannot be placed on a different space). Holds up to 2 animals of the same type (4 with a feeding trough).
Special building. Cost: 2 wood, 1 reed. Points equal half the total building materials in your supply at game end (rounded down). Cannot hold any animals. Best used when hoarding resources.
Special building. Cost: 2 wood, 1 stone. Worth 0 points. When built, immediately take 1 animal of your choice from the general supply. Holds 1 animal (2 with a feeding trough).

Special building. Cost: 3 wood or 3 stone. Worth 2 points. Functions as stables (not a stall). When built, return the stall tile it replaces to the general supply. Immediately take 1 horse or 1 cow from the supply. Holds up to 5 animals of the same type (10 with a feeding trough).

An area on your farm fully enclosed by borders and/or building edges. Each pasture holds up to 2 animals per space it covers, all of the same type. Must be fully enclosed to hold any animals. Unfinished pastures (incomplete borders) hold zero animals.

A single square on your farm board or farm expansion. Each space can hold one building or be part of a pasture. A space is 'used' if it has a building, feeding trough, or is part of a fully enclosed pasture. Used for scoring farm expansion points.
The shared pool of building materials, animals, and goods tiles in the center of the table. Resources are drawn from and returned to this supply throughout the game.
Your own stockpile of building materials (wood, stone, reed) collected from action spaces. Materials in your personal supply are spent to build fences, walls, stalls, stables, and special buildings.
The first phase of each round. Add printed goods to action spaces with refill arrows. Goods accumulate if not taken from previous rounds. Also add 1 yellow border to the Expand action space.

The second phase of each round. Players alternate placing 1 worker at a time on action spaces, starting with the start player. Each action is resolved immediately. Continues until all 6 workers are placed.
The third phase of each round. All workers return to their farms. Action spaces become available again for the next round.
The fourth and final phase of each round. For each animal type where you have 2 or more, gain 1 new animal of that type. Maximum 1 baby per type per round. Newborns with no room run away.
Action space. Take the start player tile and all wood on this space. Refill space: 1 wood added each round. Controls who goes first next round.
Action space. Take all wood from this space. Refill space: 3 wood added each round. Accumulates if unclaimed.
Action space. Take all stone from this space. Refill space: 1 stone added each round. Accumulates if unclaimed.
Action space. Take all stone from this space. Refill space: 2 stone added each round. Accumulates if unclaimed.
Action space. Take 1 wood, 1 stone, and 1 reed from the general supply. Not a refill space — always gives exactly these amounts regardless of previous rounds.
Action space. Take the reed and all sheep from this space. Refill space with conditional refill: adds 1 reed when empty, adds 1 sheep when occupied.
Action space. Place as many borders as you want from your personal supply onto your farm. Pay 1 wood per border placed.
Action space. Place as many borders as you want from your personal supply onto your farm. First 2 borders are free; pay 2 stone for each additional border.
Action space. Build exactly 1 stall on an empty farm space. Costs 3 stone and 1 reed. The stall can be placed on any farm space without an existing building, including inside a pasture.
Action space. Build 1 feeding trough for free, plus additional troughs for 3 wood each. Place on any farm space that does not already have a trough.
Action space. Upgrade 1 stall to a stable by paying 5 wood or 5 stone (not a mix). Flip the stall tile over. Can repeat this action to upgrade multiple stalls in one turn.
Action space. Take 1 farm expansion tile and attach it to your farm. Also take all yellow borders on this space (1 added per round). May not place those borders during this action.
Action space. Build 1 available special building by paying its cost and placing it on an empty farm space. Two of these spaces exist, so up to 2 special buildings can be built per round.
Action space. Take all animals from this space. Refill space: adds pigs and sheep as printed each round. Animals must be housed on your farm or they run away.
Action space. Take all animals from this space. Refill space: adds cows and pigs as printed each round. Animals must be housed on your farm or they run away.
Action space. Take all animals from this space. Refill space: adds horses and sheep as printed each round. Animals must be housed on your farm or they run away.
Each animal on your farm at game end is worth 1 point. This is the base scoring for all animal types combined.
Lose 3 points for each animal type where you have 3 or fewer at game end. Applies independently to sheep, pigs, cows, and horses. Having 0 of a type still costs only 3 points.
After reaching the bonus threshold, each additional animal of that type scores +1 extra point. Thresholds: sheep 13, pigs 11, cows 10, horses 9.
Points shown on building tiles: Half-Timbered House (5), Open Stables (2), Shelter (0), Storage Building (variable). Stalls and stables are worth 0 points themselves.
Each farm expansion where all 3 farm spaces are used scores 4 points. A space is used if it has a building, feeding trough, or is part of a fully enclosed pasture. Partially used expansions score 0.
In case of a tie, the player who was not the start player at the beginning of round 1 wins.
A building material used for fences (1 per border), feeding troughs (3 each after the first free one), stalls (not used), stables (5 for upgrade), and special buildings. 17 total in the game.
A building material used for walls (2 per border after first 2 free), stalls (3 per stall), stables (5 for upgrade), and special buildings. 15 total in the game.

A building material used for stalls (1 per stall) and certain special buildings. The scarcest resource with only 5 in the game. Primarily obtained from the Millpond and Building Materials action spaces.
One of 4 animal types. 22 in the game (most abundant). Found on Millpond, Pigs & Sheep, and Horses & Sheep action spaces. Bonus threshold: +1 per sheep past 13.
One of 4 animal types. 16 in the game. Found on Pigs & Sheep and Cows & Pigs action spaces. Bonus threshold: +1 per pig past 11.
One of 4 animal types. 13 in the game. Found on Cows & Pigs action space. Also obtained from Open Stables. Bonus threshold: +1 per cow past 10.
One of 4 animal types. 14 in the game. Found on Horses & Sheep action space. Also obtained from Open Stables. Bonus threshold: +1 per horse past 9.
